q: The bending of waves around the corners of an obstacle is defined as ___.
Diffraction also occurs when waves pass through an aperture into the region of geometrical shadow.
The amount of bending depends on the wavelength of light relative to the ___ of the aperture.
If the aperture is much larger than the wavelength of light, the bending will be ___.
Light waves diffract around objects and create bright and dark fringes.
Which object's sharp edges can be used to demonstrate light diffraction?
The two main classes of diffraction are Fresnel diffraction and ___ diffraction.
In Fresnel diffraction, the source and screen are at a ___ distance from the obstacle.
In Fraunhofer diffraction, there is an infinite distance between the source and the screen.
A single-slit diffraction pattern consists of a bright central portion called the ___.
Successive bright fringes in a single-slit pattern become ___ intense proceeding outward.
Bright and dark fringes in diffraction are created by interference of diffracted light waves.
For the first minimum in single-slit diffraction, the path difference $a \sin \theta$ is equal to ___.
Generally for the $m^{th}$ minimum in single-slit diffraction, $a \sin \theta = $ ___ .
The width of the central maximum in single-slit diffraction is $2 \times$ the ___ .
The width of the central maximum depends on the width of the slit.
A narrower slit causes a ___ central maximum.
The formula for the width of the central maximum is ___.
In diffraction by a single slit, $a$ represents the slit width.
At the central maximum, secondary wavelets reach the point $P_0$ in the ___.
